Classic Sourdough Bread

Prep: 30 min · Ferment: 12-18 hrs · Bake: 45 min · Makes: 1 loaf

Ingredients

  • 500g bread flour (12-13% protein)
  • 350g water (70% hydration)
  • 100g active sourdough starter
  • 10g fine sea salt

Equipment

  • Large mixing bowl
  • Bench scraper
  • Dutch oven (5-quart or larger)
  • Digital kitchen scale
  • Lame or razor blade

Instructions

  1. Autolyse (30 minutes)

    Mix flour and water until no dry spots remain. Cover and rest for 30 minutes. This allows the flour to fully hydrate and begins gluten development.

  2. Add Starter and Salt

    Add the sourdough starter and salt to the dough. Squeeze and fold to fully incorporate, about 3-4 minutes. The dough will feel shaggy at first but will come together.

  3. Bulk Fermentation (4-6 hours)

    Perform 4 sets of stretch and folds, spaced 30 minutes apart, during the first 2 hours. Then let the dough rest undisturbed for the remaining time. The dough should increase in volume by about 50% and show bubbles on the surface.

  4. Pre-shape

    Turn the dough out onto an unfloured surface. Using a bench scraper, gently shape into a round. Let it rest for 20 minutes.

  5. Final Shape

    Shape the dough into a tight boule or batard. Place seam-side up in a floured banneton or bowl lined with a floured towel.

  6. Cold Retard (12-18 hours)

    Cover and refrigerate overnight. This slow fermentation develops complex flavors and makes the dough easier to score.

  7. Bake

    Preheat oven to 500°F (260°C) with the Dutch oven inside for 45 minutes. Turn dough onto parchment, score with a lame, lower into Dutch oven. Bake covered 20 minutes, then uncovered 20-25 minutes until deeply golden. Internal temperature should reach 205-210°F (96-99°C).

  8. Cool

    Let the bread cool on a wire rack for at least 1 hour before slicing. Cutting too early releases steam and results in a gummy crumb.

Baker's Notes

Adjust hydration based on your flour. Whole wheat absorbs more water; pastry flour absorbs less. If your kitchen is warm (above 78°F/26°C), reduce bulk fermentation time. If cool (below 68°F/20°C), extend it or find a warmer spot.
